seoDescription: Anlogic technical support resources including documentation, EDA tools, training materials, and FAE support for FPGA development.

faqs.question: How do I get Anlogic technical documents and development tools?
faqs.answer: Download Anlogic technical documents and development tools from the official website (www.anlogic.com). Tang Dynasty software is free to download with no licensing fees. Reference designs and example projects are included in development kits or available separately. IP core libraries and interface documentation are provided to registered users. Video tutorials and online training resources help users get started quickly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Visit Anlogic website to download development tools and technical documents, or contact LiTong FAE for local support.

faqs.question: What technical support services does Anlogic provide?
faqs.answer: Anlogic provides comprehensive FPGA technical support services: 1) Device selection consultation based on application requirements, 2) Design review services for schematic and PCB optimization, 3) Code optimization support for Verilog/VHDL improvements, 4) Timing analysis guidance for timing closure, 5) Debug support with online debugging guidance, 6) Training services including FPGA design and tool usage training, 7) On-site support for critical projects. All support is provided by experienced FAE engineers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Leverage Anlogic and LiTong technical support services to accelerate FPGA product development and production.

faqs.question: What are the steps to migrate from Xilinx/Intel FPGA to Anlogic?
faqs.answer: Migration steps: 1) Device selection - choose equivalent Anlogic device based on resource usage, 2) Design import - import existing Verilog/VHDL code into Tang Dynasty software, 3) Constraint conversion - convert UCF/XDC constraints to TD format, 4) IP replacement - replace vendor IPs with Anlogic equivalents, 5) Timing optimization - optimize critical paths based on timing reports, 6) Hardware verification - validate functionality using Anlogic development boards, 7) Production import - finalize design for mass production. Anlogic FAE provides full migration support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong FAE for detailed migration guide and one-on-one technical support services.

faqs.question: What tools and hardware are needed for Anlogic FPGA development?
faqs.answer: Basic requirements: 1) Development software - Tang Dynasty (TD) free EDA tool supporting complete design flow, 2) Programmer - USB programmer for connecting PC to FPGA board, 3) Development board - select appropriate kit like ELF2-DK or EAGLE-DK, 4) Power supply - typically 5V or 12V DC, 5) PC configuration - Windows 10/11, 8GB+ RAM, 100GB+ storage. Anlogic provides complete development kits including software, programmer, board, and documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Purchase Anlogic development kit for complete tool chain, or download free TD software to start design.

faqs.question: What IP cores are available from Anlogic?
faqs.answer: Anlogic provides rich IP core libraries: 1) Interface IP - PCIe Gen3, DDR3/4 controllers, Ethernet MAC (10M/100M/1G/10G), USB 2.0/3.0, SATA, SPI, I2C, UART, 2) Digital signal processing IP - FFT/IFFT, FIR filters, CORDIC, multipliers, dividers, 3) Video processing IP - image scaling, color space conversion, video overlay, de-interlacing, 4) Communication protocol IP - CPRI, eCPRI, Interlaken, Aurora, 5) Security IP - AES, SHA, RSA encryption/decryption. Most IP cores are free with detailed documentation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse IP Catalog in Tang Dynasty software, select appropriate IP cores and integrate following documentation.

faqs.question: How to solve timing issues in Anlogic FPGA designs?
faqs.answer: Common methods to resolve timing issues: 1) Constraint settings - ensure all clocks and I/O are properly constrained, 2) Code optimization - avoid long combinational logic, insert pipeline registers, optimize state machines, 3) Resource optimization - properly use DSP and RAM resources, avoid routing congestion, 4) Placement optimization - use placement constraints for critical modules, 5) Clock optimization - use clock enables instead of gated clocks, plan clock domains properly, 6) Iterative optimization - analyze critical paths from timing reports and optimize accordingly.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow FPGA design best practices, use TD software timing analysis tools, seek FAE support when needed.

faqs.question: How to estimate and optimize power consumption?
faqs.answer: Power estimation and optimization: 1) Use Tang Dynasty power analysis tool to estimate static and dynamic power, 2) Static power optimization - select low-power devices, reduce operating voltage, use low-power modes, 3) Dynamic power optimization - reduce clock frequency, use clock gating, optimize signal toggle rates, 4) Resource optimization - properly use DSP and RAM, avoid resource waste, 5) I/O optimization - use low-voltage I/O standards, reduce I/O toggling. ELF series inherently uses low-power architecture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TD software power analysis tool for estimation, follow low-power design principles.

faqs.question: How is reliability ensured for Anlogic FPGAs?
faqs.answer: Reliability assurance measures: 1) Device grades - commercial (0-70C) and industrial (-40-85C) temperature grades available, 2) Quality certification - ISO 9001 quality management, JEDEC standard compliance, 3) Test coverage - 100% functional testing, burn-in testing, temperature cycling, 4) Supply assurance - domestic devices with secure supply chain, 5) Design support - reliability design guides including decoupling, reset circuits, ESD protection, 6) Failure analysis - failure analysis services provided. Anlogic FPGAs are proven in industrial, communication, and automotive applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select appropriate temperature grade based on environment, follow reliability design guides.

articles.title: Anlogic FPGA Selection Guide
articles.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting the right Anlogic FPGA device based on application requirements
articles.shortDescription: How to choose the right Anlogic FPGA device for your application
articles.summary: This guide covers Anlogic FPGA product families, logic capacity assessment, I/O requirements analysis, memory and DSP needs, and power considerations to help engineers select the optimal device.

contentSections.heading: Understanding Anlogic FPGA Product Families
contentSections.content: Anlogic offers two main FPGA product families: ELF Series and EAGLE Series. ELF series are low-power, low-cost FPGAs using 55nm process with 1K-10K LUTs and embedded Flash, ideal for industrial control, consumer electronics, and communication interfaces. EAGLE series are high-performance FPGAs using 28nm process with 10K-100K+ LUTs and high-speed SerDes transceivers, supporting PCIe Gen3, DDR3/4, and 10G Ethernet for communication infrastructure, video processing, and AI acceleration.

contentSections.heading: Evaluating Logic Capacity Requirements
contentSections.content: The primary factor in FPGA selection is logic capacity. Assessment methods: 1) Count registers, combinational logic complexity, and state machine scale in your design, 2) Consider IP core resource usage like PCIe hard IP and DDR controllers, 3) Reserve 20-30% margin for future expansion, 4) Reference similar designs. General guidance: simple control applications choose 1K-5K LUTs (ELF2L15/ELF2L45), medium complexity designs choose 5K-20K LUTs (ELF2L90/EG4S20), high-performance computing choose 20K+ LUTs (EG4S50/EG4S100).

contentSections.heading: Determining I/O Requirements
contentSections.content: I/O requirement assessment includes: 1) Quantity - count all external interface pins including data, address, control, clock, and debug signals, 2) Voltage standards - determine system voltage levels like 3.3V LVCMOS, 2.5V, 1.8V, LVDS, MIPI, etc., 3) Speed - high-speed interfaces like DDR and SerDes require special I/O support, 4) Package - select QFN, BGA based on I/O count and PCB space. Note: Different packages have different I/O counts and distributions.

contentSections.heading: Considering Special Function Requirements
contentSections.content: Determine special function needs based on application: 1) High-speed interfaces - choose EAGLE series for SerDes requiring >5Gbps, supporting 10Gbps+ rates, 2) Memory interfaces - DDR3/4 control requires sufficient I/O and clock resources, 3) PCIe interfaces - EAGLE series integrates PCIe hard IP to simplify design, 4) DSP functions - count multiplier requirements and select devices with sufficient DSP slices, 5) Temperature range - choose industrial grade (-40 to 85C) for industrial environments.

faqs.question: How to choose between ELF and EAGLE series?
faqs.answer: Choose EAGLE series for high-speed SerDes and PCIe interface requirements, ELF series for general applications. EAGLE for communication and video processing, ELF for industrial control and consumer electronics.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select based on high-speed interface needs. EAGLE for SerDes/PCIe, ELF for most medium-speed applications.

faqs.question: How to estimate required LUT count?
faqs.answer: Estimate LUT count by counting always blocks, combinational logic complexity, and state machine states in Verilog code. General experience: simple controllers 1-2K LUTs, communication protocol processing 3-5K LUTs, video processing 5-10K LUTs, complex algorithms 10K+ LUTs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Reference similar design resource usage, reserve 20-30% margin, validate with development kit if uncertain.

faqs.question: Which Xilinx devices can Anlogic FPGAs replace?
faqs.answer: ELF2L45 can replace Spartan-6 XC6SLX9, Spartan-7 XC7S25. ELF2L90 can replace XC6SLX25, XC7S50. EG4S20 can replace Artix-7 XC7A35T. Anlogic provides detailed replacement comparison tables and migration guides.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong FAE for replacement comparison tables and migration support.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: Device selection is the critical first step for FPGA design success. Common mistakes include over-designing with oversized devices wasting cost, insufficient I/O requiring board redesign, and ignoring temperature grades causing failures in industrial environments. Recommendations: validate design with development kit first, reserve appropriate margin without over-designing, carefully check I/O requirements and package pinout.
keyTakeaways: Carefully verify I/O count and package
Validate with development kit before finalizing

customerCases.challenge: Needed to select appropriate FPGA for new PLC product line with low cost and stable supply
customerCases.solution: Recommended ELF2L45B based on I/O requirements and logic complexity, provided Xilinx replacement analysis
customerCases.result: Successfully replaced original Xilinx solution, 40% cost reduction, stable supply

articles.seoTitle: Anlogic FPGA Selection Guide - Device Selection | LiTong Distributor
articles.seoDescription: Anlogic FPGA selection guide: ELF and EAGLE series selection criteria, logic capacity assessment, I/O requirements analysis, package selection recommendations.

articles.title: Tang Dynasty Software Tutorial
articles.description: Step-by-step tutorial for using Tang Dynasty development software for FPGA development
articles.shortDescription: Getting started with Tang Dynasty FPGA design software
articles.summary: Complete tutorial covering Tang Dynasty software installation, project creation, design entry, synthesis, implementation, and debugging for Anlogic FPGA development.

contentSections.heading: Installation and Setup
contentSections.content: Tang Dynasty (TD) software installation steps: 1) Download latest version from Anlogic website, supporting Windows and Linux, 2) Run installer following wizard, avoid Chinese characters and spaces in path, 3) Install USB programmer driver, system will auto-recognize when connected, 4) Launch software and select working directory, 5) Install device support packages for target FPGA, 6) Configure preferences like editor font and theme. Software is completely free with no license required.

contentSections.heading: Creating and Managing Projects
contentSections.content: TD software project management: 1) Create new project: File -> New Project, enter name and path, select target device, 2) Add source files: Right-click project -> Add Files, supports Verilog (.v), VHDL (.vhd), constraint files (.adc), 3) Project settings: Right-click -> Properties, configure synthesis options and timing constraints, 4) File organization: categorize source, constraint, and IP files, 5) Version control: project files (.prj) and sources need version control, generated files don't.

contentSections.heading: Design Entry and Synthesis
contentSections.content: Design entry and synthesis flow: 1) Code editing: TD built-in editor with syntax highlighting, or use external editors like VS Code, 2) Write RTL code: use Verilog or VHDL following synthesizable coding guidelines, 3) Add constraints: create constraint file defining clocks, I/O locations, voltage standards, 4) Run synthesis: click synthesis button or F7, converts RTL to gate-level netlist, 5) Review reports: check resource usage and timing reports. Common issues: syntax errors shown in message window, latch warnings from incomplete if/case statements.

contentSections.heading: Implementation and Debugging
contentSections.content: Implementation and debugging steps: 1) Place and route: click implement button for placement and routing, generating bitstream, 2) Timing analysis: review timing reports ensuring setup and hold requirements met, 3) Download configuration: connect USB programmer, click download button, select bitstream file, 4) Online debugging: use built-in Logic Analyzer to capture internal signals with trigger conditions, 5) Permanent configuration: write bitstream to FPGA internal Flash for auto-load on power-up.

faqs.question: Is Tang Dynasty software free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tang Dynasty software is completely free with no licensing fees, no functional limitations, and unlimited usage period. Supports Windows and Linux, free download from Anlogic website.
faqs.decisionGuide: Free to download and use, no licensing concerns, suitable for enterprise and individual developers.

faqs.question: How does TD compare to Xilinx Vivado/ISE?
faqs.answer: TD interface is similar to ISE, operation logic comparable to mainstream FPGA tools. Engineers with Xilinx/Intel experience can quickly adapt. Main differences: TD is completely free, optimized for Anlogic devices, faster startup and compilation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Engineers with FPGA experience can adapt to TD software in 1-2 days, low learning curve.

faqs.question: How to perform online debugging?
faqs.answer: TD software integrates Logic Analyzer functionality. Insert ILA core in design, set trigger conditions and observation signals, capture waveforms in real-time after download. No additional logic analyzer hardware needed, reducing debug costs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use built-in Logic Analyzer for online debugging, set appropriate trigger conditions to capture key signals.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: Tang Dynasty is the core tool for Anlogic FPGA development. While not as comprehensive as Vivado, it covers most application needs. Usage recommendations: 1) Learn keyboard shortcuts for efficiency, 2) Use template projects for quick starts, 3) Pay attention to warnings in synthesis and implementation reports, 4) Learn to use Logic Analyzer for debugging. Contact FAE support for issues.
keyTakeaways: Use template projects for quick starts

customerCases.challenge: Students learning FPGA development need free development tools
customerCases.solution: Recommended Anlogic FPGA and Tang Dynasty software, completely free for educational use
customerCases.result: Students successfully completed FPGA course projects, mastered programmable logic design skills

articles.seoTitle: Tang Dynasty Tutorial - FPGA Development Tool Guide | LiTong Distributor
articles.seoDescription: Complete Tang Dynasty software tutorial: installation, project management, design entry, synthesis, implementation, debugging. Free FPGA development tool guide.

articles.title: FPGA Design Best Practices
articles.description: Best practices for designing with Anlogic FPGAs including coding guidelines, timing constraints, and optimization techniques
articles.shortDescription: Best practices for efficient FPGA design with Anlogic devices
articles.summary: This article summarizes best practices for Anlogic FPGA design covering Verilog/VHDL coding standards, timing constraint setup, power optimization techniques, and debugging methods to improve design quality and efficiency.

contentSections.heading: Coding Standards and Style
contentSections.content: Good coding standards are the foundation of successful FPGA design. Use synchronous design methods with all sequential logic clocked on clock edges. Avoid gated clocks and latches, use clock enables instead. Module partitioning should be reasonable with single functionality and clear interfaces. Signal naming should be meaningful with consistent naming conventions. Code should include sufficient comments explaining design intent and key logic.

contentSections.heading: Timing Constraints and Optimization
contentSections.content: Timing constraints are critical for FPGA design. Must define clock constraints for all clocks and input/output delay constraints for I/O. Use multi-cycle path constraints and false path constraints for optimization. For critical paths, improve performance through pipeline optimization, parallel processing, and resource sharing. Regularly check timing reports ensuring setup and hold times are met.

contentSections.heading: Power Optimization Techniques
contentSections.content: Power optimization should be considered from the early design stage. Use clock gating to disable idle modules, reducing dynamic power. Select appropriate operating frequency avoiding excessive clock speeds. Optimize algorithms and data paths reducing signal toggling. Use low-power modes during idle periods. Select appropriate operating voltage balancing performance and power.

faqs.question: How to avoid metastability issues?
faqs.answer: Use synchronizers for clock domain crossing signals, at least two-stage flip-flop synchronization. For single-bit signals use dual flip-flop synchronizers, for multi-bit signals use FIFO or handshake protocols. Avoid combinational logic on clock domain crossing paths.
faqs.decisionGuide: Clock domain crossing signals must use synchronizers, recommend two-stage flip-flop synchronization.

faqs.question: What to do when timing is not met?
faqs.answer: When timing is not met, first check if timing constraints are correct. Then optimize code: insert pipeline registers to break long paths, optimize critical path logic, use faster implementation strategies. If necessary reduce clock frequency or switch to faster speed grade devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: When timing not met, check constraints first, then optimize code, finally consider frequency reduction or device change.

faqs.question: How to reduce power consumption?
faqs.answer: Methods to reduce power: use clock gating, reduce operating frequency, optimize algorithms reducing toggling, use low-power modes, select low-power devices. Consider power budget from early design stage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Approach power reduction from architecture design, code optimization, and tool settings.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: Design best practices are summaries of years of project experience. We have seen too many cases of project delays or failures due to neglecting coding standards and timing constraints. We recommend all FPGA engineers take these best practices seriously from project start. Good design habits can multiply effectiveness and avoid extensive debug time later.
keyTakeaways: Take coding standards and design habits seriously
Timing constraints are key to design success
Power optimization starts from architecture design

customerCases.challenge: FPGA design timing difficult to close, project delayed
customerCases.solution: Redesigned following best practices, optimized timing constraints and code
customerCases.result: Timing successfully closed, project delivered on schedule

articles.seoTitle: FPGA Design Best Practices - Coding Standards and Timing | LiTong Distributor

articles.title: Xilinx to Anlogic FPGA Migration Guide
articles.description: Complete guide for migrating Xilinx FPGA designs to Anlogic FPGAs including device selection, code porting, and constraint conversion
articles.shortDescription: Complete guide for migrating from Xilinx to Anlogic FPGAs
articles.summary: This guide provides complete instructions for migrating from Xilinx FPGA to Anlogic FPGA including device selection comparison, Verilog/VHDL code porting, timing constraint conversion, IP replacement, and debugging techniques.

contentSections.heading: Device Selection Comparison
contentSections.content: First step in migration is selecting appropriate Anlogic device to replace original Xilinx device. ELF2L45 can replace Spartan-6 XC6SLX9, Spartan-7 XC7S25. ELF2L90 can replace XC6SLX25, XC7S50. EG4S20 can replace Artix-7 XC7A35T. Consider logic capacity, I/O count, and special functions like SerDes and PCIe. Anlogic provides detailed replacement comparison tables, contact FAE for assistance.

contentSections.heading: Code Porting Points
contentSections.content: Most Verilog/VHDL code can be used directly on Anlogic FPGA. Points to note: 1) Avoid Xilinx-specific primitives and IPs, replace with Anlogic equivalents, 2) Clock management modules (DCM/PLL) need reconfiguration, 3) Memory (BRAM) ports and timing may differ slightly, 4) DSP module usage is similar but parameters may need adjustment. Compile step by step in Tang Dynasty software, resolving errors and warnings.

contentSections.heading: Constraint Conversion Methods
contentSections.content: Xilinx UCF/XDC constraints need conversion to Tang Dynasty supported format. Clock constraints, I/O location constraints, and timing constraints have similar syntax but need adjustment. Tang Dynasty provides constraint conversion tools for automatic conversion of most constraints. Special constraints may need manual adjustment. Recommend converting basic constraints first, then optimizing based on compilation results.

faqs.question: How long does migration take?
faqs.answer: Simple designs 1-2 weeks, medium complexity 2-4 weeks, complex designs 1-2 months. Time depends on design complexity, IP usage, and team experience. Anlogic FAE can provide migration support to shorten time.
faqs.decisionGuide: Assess migration workload in advance, allow sufficient time, seek FAE support when needed.

faqs.question: How to replace Xilinx IPs?
faqs.answer: Xilinx IPs need replacement with Anlogic equivalents or custom implementation. Common IPs like FIFO, RAM, PLL have corresponding implementations. Complex IPs like PCIe, DDR controllers use Anlogic hard IP or soft IP. Anlogic provides IP replacement guides and reference designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Anlogic equivalent IPs to replace Xilinx IPs, complex IPs reference Anlogic reference designs.

faqs.question: Are pins compatible?
faqs.answer: Some models support pin-to-pin compatibility for direct replacement. Incompatible models require PCB redesign. Anlogic provides pin comparison tables to assess compatibility. Recommend confirming pin compatibility before migration.
faqs.decisionGuide: Confirm pin compatibility, compatible models for direct replacement, incompatible requires PCB redesign.

faeInsights.author: LiTong FAE Team
faeInsights.content: We have helped dozens of customers successfully migrate from Xilinx to Anlogic FPGA. Key success factors: 1) Thorough pre-assessment confirming device replacement feasibility, 2) Phased migration with simple modules first, 3) Fully utilizing Anlogic reference designs and FAE support, 4) Comprehensive testing and verification. Migration is not only a technical issue but also an important supply chain management decision.
keyTakeaways: Utilize reference designs and FAE support

customerCases.challenge: Needed to migrate Xilinx Spartan-6 design to domestic FPGA
customerCases.solution: Used Anlogic ELF2L45B replacement with FAE full migration support
customerCases.result: Completed migration in 4 weeks, 40% cost reduction, performance met requirements
